title: 第 36 節
en: 5 Ibid., X, 17, 3, pusha tva itaft &yavayatu pra vidvan — saA tva etebhyaA pari dadat pitribhyaA.

they attained the highest supremacy. Mitra is greater than the earth and the sky1, he supports even all the gods2. Vishnu supports all the worlds3; he is the companion of Indra in his battles4, and no one can reach the limits of his greatness5.
The sun in a secondary position.

If we knew nothing else of the religious poetry of the Veda, we might, after reading such praises bestowed upon the sun, feel inclined to say that the old Brahmans worshipped the sun under various names as their supreme deity ; and that in that sense they might be said to worship one god only, to be in fact, monotheists. Nothing, however, could be further from the truth. In this one evolution, no doubt, the sun assumed the character of a supreme deity, but even in the passages which we have quoted there is hardly an assertion of the sun's supremacy that could not be matched in 1 Rig- Veda, III, 59, 7, abhi yah mahina divam mitra/i babhuva sapratha/i, abhi sravobhi^ prithivim.
2 Ibid., Ill, 59, 8, s&h devan visvan bibharti.
3 Ibid., I, 154, 4, y&h u tridhatu przthivim uta dyam, eka/t dadhara bhuvanani visva, He who in three places supports the earth and the sky, who alone supports all beings.

4 Rig- Veda, VI, 69. 6 Ibid., VII, 99, 2, na te vishno ^ayamanaA na gatah, deva mahimnaA param antam apa, astabhnaA nakam rishvam brihantam, dadhartha pra&im kakubham prithivyaA. No one who is now living or who lived formerly reached, O Deva, the furthest end of tliy greatness ;
Thou hast supported the sky, the bright and great, thou hast holden the eastern point of the earth.

HENOTHEISM, POLYTHEISM, MONOTHEISM, ATHEISM. 271 the hymns addressed to other Devas. He is totally different in that respect from Zeus and Jupiter. Nor do the Vedic poets hesitate for a moment to represent the same deity, the sun, who is at one time the maker and upholder of all things, at another time as the child of the waters, as pro duced by the dawns, a god among other gods, neither better nor worse.

This is the peculiar character of the ancient Vedic religion which I have tried to characterise as Henotheism or Katkenotheism, a successive belief in single supreme gods, in order to keep it distinct from that phase of religious thought which we commonly call polytheism, in which the many gods are already subordinated to one supreme god, and by which therefore the craving after the one without a second, has been more fully satisfied. In the Veda one god after another is invoked. For the time being, all that can be said of a divine being is ascribed to him. The poet, while addressing him, seems hardly to know of any other gods. But in the same collection of hymns, sometimes even in the same hymn, other gods are mentioned, and they also are truly divine, truly independent, or, it may be, supreme. The vision of the worshipper seems to change suddenly, and the same poet who at one moment saw nothing but the sun, as the ruler of heaven and earth, now sees heaven and earth, as the father and mother of the sun and of all the gods.

It may be difficult for us to enter into this phase of religious thought, but it is a phase perfectly intelligible, nay inevitable, if only we remember that the idea of deity, as we understand it, was 272 LECTURE VI.

not yet fixed and settled, but was only slowly growing towards perfection. The poets ascribed the highest powers to the sun, but they ascribed equally high powers to other natural phenomena likewise. It was their object to praise the moun tains, the trees, the rivers, the earth and the sky, the storm and the fire, as high as ever they could be praised. By these superlative praises eachbecame in turn a superlative or supreme power ; but to say that they represented each and all as gods or even as devas, involves a mental anachro nism, for, when they first uttered those praises, they did not yet possess either that word or that idea. They were looking, no doubt, for something in all these phenomena, which afterwards they called divine. But at first they had to be satisfied with predicating of the various objects of their praise the highest they could predicate. After having done that, nay while doing it, some of the predicates which were applicable to all or most of the objects of their praise would assume an inde pendent character, and thus supply the first names and conceptions of what we call divine. If the moun tains, the rivers, the sky, and the sun, were all called living and doing (asura), not-perishing (a^ara), immortal (amartya), or bright (deva), then each of these predicates would, after a time, become the name of a class of beings, expressing not only their vital vigour, their freedom, from decay or their brilliancy, but everything else that was connoted by these words. To say that Agni or fire belongs to the devas or bright beings would then be something very different from saying that fire is bright. To say that Dyaus, the sky, or Surya, HENOTHEISM, POLYTHEISM, MONOTHEISM, ATHEISM. 273 the sun, is an asura (a living one) or an amartya (immortal) would imply far more than that the sky does not fade away, or that he is active and moving about. These general predicates, such as asura, vigorous, a^ara, imperishable, deva, bright, always predicate one arid the same thing of many objects ; and if the upholders of an original monotheism mean no more than this, that the predicate god which is looked for and slowly conquered, that the intention of the divine, is by its very nature one, there might be something to be said for such a theory.

But what interests us at present, is how that intention was realized ; by how many steps, by how many names, the infinite was grasped, the unknown named, and at last the Divine reached. Those beings who are called devas in the Veda are in many places not yet even the same as the Greek Oeo/; for the Greeks, even so early as the time of Homer, had begun to suspect that, whatever the number and nature of the so-called gods might be, there must be something supreme, whether a god or a fate, there must be at least one father of gods and men. In some portions of the Yeda, too, the same idea breaks through, and we imagine that as in Greece, Italy, Germany, and else where, so in India also, the religious craving after the one would have been satisfied by a monar chical polytheism. But the Indian mind soon went further, and we shall see how in the end it was driven to a denial of all the devas or gods, and to search for something higher than all the devas, Dyaus himself, or Varuwa, or Indra, or Pra^apati not excluded. At present, when dealing with the 274 LECTURE VI, genesis of the Vedic gods or devas, what I want chiefly to show is that beginning from different beginnings, nothing is more natural than that they should grow up at first side by side, unconcerned about each other, each perfect in his own sphere, and that sphere for a time filling the whole horizon of the vision of their worshippers.

Herein lies the interest and chief value of the Vedic hyrnns, only that it is almost impossible to exhibit the fulness of those thoughts in modern language. When the poets of the Veda address the mountains to protect them, when they implore the rivers to yield them water, they may speak of rivers and mountains as devas, but even then, though deva would be more than bright, it would as yet be very far from anything we mean by divine. How then shall we do justice to the old language and its real vagueness by our translation into sharply defined modern terms \ To the Vedic poets the rivers and mountains were, no doubt, the same as they are to us, but they were conceived more prominently as active, because everything which in their language was comprehended by a name coul.l only be comprehended as manifesting some activity of which man was conscious in himself; it had no interest, it had no existence in their minds, except when conceived as active. But there is still a long way from this conception of certain parts of nature as active, to what is called personification or deification. Even when the poets spoke of the sun as standing on a chariot, as clad in golden armour, as spreading out his arms, this was no more than a poetical perception of something in nature that reminded them of HENOTHEISM, POLYTHEISM, MONOTHEISM, ATHEISM. 275 their own proceedings. What to us is poetry, was to them prose. What to us seems fantastic imagery, arose more often from helplessness in grasping, and poverty in naming the surrounding world than from any desire of startling or pleasing their hearers. If we could ask Vasish^a or Visvamitra, or any of the old Aryan poets, whether they really thought that the sun, the golden ball which they saw, was a man with legs and arms, with a heart and lungs, they would no doubt laugh at us, and tell us that though we understood their language, we did not understand their thoughts.

A word like SavitH, the sun, meant at first no more than what it said. It was derived from the root su, to bring forth, to give life, and therefore, when applied to the sun, it meant just so much of the sun as was perceived of him in his acts of lifegiving and fertilizing, and no more. Afterwards only, Savitrz became on one hand the name of a mythological being of whom certain stories, applicable to the vivifying sun, might be told ; while on the other hand Savitrz' dwindled away into a traditional and unmeaning word for sun.

The process which we have been watching in the case of the sun, we can watch again and again with regard to most Vedic deities. Not, however, with regard to all. The so-called semi-deities, the rivers, the mountains, the clouds, the sea, others also such as the dawn, the night, the wind, or the storm, never rise to the rank of supreme deity ; but of Agni, the fire, of Varuwa, the covering sky, of Indra, Vishwu, Rudra, Soma, Pan/anya, and others, epithets are used and whole descriptions given which, to our T 2 276 LECTURE VI.
mi rid, would be appropriate to a supreme deity only.
The sky as Dyaus, or the illuminator.

Let us look at the origin and history of one other god, one of the oldest gods, not only of the Vedic Aryans, but of the whole Aryan race, I mean the Vedic Dyaus, the Greek ZeJ?. Some scholars seem still to doubt the existence of such a deity in the Veda, and there is certainly no trace of Dyaus as a god, nay, even as a masculine noun, in the later literature of India. Dyaus has there become a feminine, and means simply the sky. Now it has always seemed to me one of the most wonderful dis coveries made by the students of the Veda that a deity, which was known to have existed in Greece as Zeu? irarrjp, in Italy as Ju-piter, in the Edda as Tyr, in German as Zio, and which we know ought to have existed in Sanskrit also, but which did not exist there, should suddenly have come to light in these ancient hymns of the Veda. In the Veda Dyaus occurs, not only as a masculine, but in that close connection with pita, father, as Dyaushpita, which we find again in the Latin Jupiter. This discovery of Dyaush-pita was like finding at last, by means of a powerful telescope, the very star in the very place of the heavens, which we had fixed before by calculation.

However, even in the Veda, Dyaus is already a fading star. The meaning of the word is generally given as sky, but its truer meaning would be ' the bright or the shining one,' for it is derived from the root div or dyu, to shine, to lighten ; and it was this activity of shining and illuminating the world which was embodied in the name of Dyaus. Who the HENOTHEISM, POLYTHEISM, MONOTHEISM, ATHEISM. 277 shining one was, the word by itself did not declare. He was an asura, a living one ; that was all. After wards only, Dyaus became the centre of mythological stories, while in the ordinary language it dwindled away, just like Savitr^, the life-giver, into one of the many traditional and unmeaning words for sky.

This Dyaus, then, the light, or the illuminator of the sky, was no doubt, from the very first, pre eminently fitted to assume some kind of supremacy among the other devas or bright beings ; and we know how completely that supremacy was realized in the Greek ZeJ? and the Latin Jupiter. In the Vedic Dyaus, too, we can watch the same tendency ; but it was there counteracted by that tendency in herent in almost every Deva to assume a superlative character.

Dyaus, the sky, is frequently invoked together with the Earth, and with Fire. For instance (RigVeda, VI, 51, 5), ' Dyaus (sky), father, and Pn'thivl (earth), kind mother, Agni (fire), brother, ye Vasus, ye bright ones, have mercy upon us ! '

Dyaus, we see, occupies the first place, and so he does generally in these old invocations. He is constantly called father. For instance (I, 191, 6), 1 D}^aus is father, Pr^thivi, the earth, your mother ; Soma, your brother ; Aditi, your sister.' Or again (Rig-Veda, IV, i; 10), Dyaus, the father, the creator, Dyaush pit& (/anita, Zei/9 Trarrip yeverrjp.

More frequently, however, than by himself, Dyaus (the sky) is invoked together with Prithivl, the earth ; and the two words, joined together, form a kind of dual deity in the Veda, called Dyavapn'thivi, Heaven and Earth.

Now, there are many passages in the Veda where Heaven and Earth are invoked as supreme deities. Thus the gods are said to be their sons1, more par ticularly the two most popular deities in the Veda, Indra2 and Agni3, are mentioned as their offspring. It is they, the two parents, who have made the world4, who protect it5, who support by their power everything, whatsoever exists6.

Yet, after heaven and earth have received every epithet that can be invented to express their imperishableness, their omnipotence, their eternity, we suddenly hear of a clever workman among the gods who made heaven and earth, whether called Dyavdprithivi7 or Kodasi8. In some places Indra is said to have produced and to support heaven and earth9, the same Indra who elsewhere is represented as the son of Dyaus, or as the son of heaven and earth10.
Struggle for supremacy between Dyaus and Indra. In fact we see here for the first time some kind of 1 Rig- Veda, I, 159, i, devaputre. 2 Ibid., IV, 17.
3 Ibid., X, 2, 7, yam tva dyavapn'thivi yam tva apaA, tvashia yam tva sugrani ma gagana.
4 Ibid., I, 159, 2, suretasa pitara bhuma kakraiuh.
5 Ibid., I, 1 60, 2, pita mata ka bhuvanani rakshataA.
zh: 5 同上，X, 17, 3：*puṣā tvā itaḥ āyavayatu pra vidvān — saḥ tvā etebhyaḥ pari dadat pitṛbhyaḥ*。
他們達到了最高的至高地位。密陀羅（Mitra）大於地與天¹，他甚至支撐著所有眾神²。毗濕奴（Vishnu）支撐著所有世界³；他是因陀羅（Indra）徵戰中的同伴⁴，無人能及他偉大的極限⁵。
太陽在次要位置上。

如果我們對吠陀的宗教詩歌一無所知，那麼在讀過這般對太陽的頌讚之後，或許會傾向於認為：古婆羅門以各種名義崇拜太陽，將其視為至高神；就此意義而言，他們可說只崇拜一位神，事實上可被稱為一神論者。然而，再沒有比這更偏離真相的了。在這一演化脈絡中，太陽無疑被賦予了至高神的性格，但即使在我們所引用的段落中，也幾乎找不到任何關於太陽至高無上的宣稱，是無法在《梨俱吠陀》第三卷第五十九頌第七句中找到對應的：「祂以偉大遍覆天穹，以榮光遍覆大地。」
2 同上，III, 59, 8，s&h devan visvan bibharti。
3 Ibid., I, 154, 4, y&h u tridhatu przthivim uta dyam, eka/t dadhara bhuvanani visva，祂在三處支撐大地與天空，獨自維繫一切眾生。

4 梨俱吠陀，VI，69。6 同上，VII，99，2：*na te vishno jāyamāno na gataḥ, deva mahimnaḥ param antam apa, astabhnaḥ nakam ṛṣvam bṛhantam, dadhartha prācīm kakubham pṛthivyāḥ*。無論是現今活著的人，還是過去曾活著的人，啊，提婆（Deva），都未曾達到你偉大的極致；
你支撐了蒼穹，明亮而偉大，你持守了大地東方的極點。
那些獻給其他提婆（Devas）的讚歌也是如此。在這一點上，他與宙斯（Zeus）和朱庇特（Jupiter）截然不同。吠陀詩人亦毫不猶豫地將同一位神祇——太陽——有時描繪為萬物的創造者與維繫者，有時又將其刻畫為水之子、由黎明所生，不過是眾神中的一位神，既不優越也不遜色。

這是古吠陀宗教的獨特特徵，我試圖將其界定為「單一神教」（Henotheism）或「輪換主神教」（Kathenotheism），亦即一種接續信仰單一至高神的模式，目的在於將它與我們通常稱為「多神教」（polytheism）的宗教思想階段區分開來；在多神教中，眾神已然從屬於一位至高神，因此對「獨一無二者」的渴求得到了更充分的滿足。在《吠陀》中，一位又一位的神祇被召喚。在當下那一刻，凡能歸於神聖存有的一切屬性，皆被賦予祂。詩人在向祂祈禱時，幾乎彷彿不知有其他神祇存在。然而，在同一部頌詩集中，有時甚至在同一首頌詩裡，其他神祇也會被提及，而他們同樣是真正神聖、真正獨立，甚至可能是至高無上的。崇拜者的視野似乎驟然轉變：那位前一瞬間眼中唯有太陽、視其為天地統治者的詩人，此刻卻將天地視為太陽與眾神之父與母。
我們或許難以進入這種宗教思想的階段，但這是一個完全可以理解的階段，甚至可以說是無可避免的——只要我們記得，我們所理解的「神性」概念，

尚未固定與確定，而是正緩慢地趨向完善。詩人們將至高權能歸於太陽，但他們也同樣將至高權能歸於其他自然現象。他們的目標是盡其所能地讚頌山巒、樹木、河流、大地與天空、風暴與火焰。透過這些極致的讚頌，每一者輪流成為一種至高或無上的力量；但若說他們將每一者與所有這些都表現為神（gods）甚至提婆（devas），則涉及一種心智上的時代錯置，因為在他們最初發出這些讚頌時，他們尚未擁有那個詞語或那個觀念。無疑地，他們在所有這些現象中尋找某種東西，後來他們稱之為神聖。但起初，他們只能滿足於將他們所能預測的最高屬性，加諸於他們所讚頌的各種對象之上。在完成此事之後，甚至在此過程中，某些適用於他們所讚頌之全部或大部分對象的屬性，便會呈現出一種獨立的特質，從而提供了我們所謂神聖之物的最初名稱與概念。如果山巒、河流、天空與太陽都被稱為「活著的與行動的」（asura）、「不滅的」（a^ara）、「不朽的」（amartya），或「光明的」（deva），那麼這些屬性中的每一項，經過一段時間後，都會成為某一類存有者的名稱，不僅表達其生命活力、免於腐朽或光輝燦爛，也表達這些詞語所涵蓋的一切其他意涵。說阿耆尼（Agni）或火焰屬於提婆（devas）或光明存有者，與說火焰是光明的，便有了天壤之別。說特尤斯（Dyaus），即天空，或蘇利耶（Surya），即太陽，是一位阿修羅（asura，活著的存有者）或一位阿摩塔（amartya，不朽者），其所蘊含的意義遠超過天空不會消逝，或他是活躍且移動的。這些普遍的屬性，例如 asura（強健的）、a^ara（不滅的）、deva（光明的），總是對許多對象預測同一件事；而如果原始一神論的支持者所意指的不過是：那個被尋找且緩慢獲得的「神」這個屬性，亦即神聖的意圖，就其本質而言是單一的，那麼這種理論或許還有一些可說之處。

但我們目前所關心的，是那意圖如何實現；經由多少步驟、透過多少名稱，無限者被掌握、未知者被命名，最終神聖者得以觸及。在《吠陀》中被稱為「提婆」（devas）的那些存在，在許多地方甚至還不完全等同於希臘文的 θεοί；因為希臘人早在荷馬時代就已開始懷疑，無論那些所謂眾神的數量與本質為何，必定存在某種至高者——無論是神或命運——至少有一位神與人之父。在《吠陀》的某些部分，同樣的想法也浮現出來，我們可以想像，正如在希臘、義大利、日耳曼及其他地方，在印度也是如此，對「一」的宗教渴望本可透過一種君主式多神教（monarchical polytheism）得到滿足。然而印度心靈很快便更進一步，我們將看到它最終如何被迫否定所有提婆或眾神，轉而尋求比一切提婆更高者——連特尤斯（Dyaus）本身、伐樓那（Varuṇa）、因陀羅（Indra）或生主（Prajāpati）也不例外。此刻，在處理吠陀諸神或提婆的起源時，我主要想指出的是：從不同的起點出發，最自然不過的是它們起初並肩成長，彼此互不相干，各自在其領域中臻於完美，而那領域在一個時期內，充滿了其崇拜者視野中的整個地平線。

吠陀頌詩的趣味與主要價值正在於此，只是要以現代語言充分展現那些思想的豐厚內涵幾乎不可能。當吠陀詩人向群山呼籲保護他們，當他們懇求河流賜予他們水源時，他們或許稱河流與群山為提婆（devas），但即便如此，儘管提婆（deva）的意涵已超越「明亮」，距離我們所謂「神聖」仍相去甚遠。那麼，我們該如何以界定分明的現代術語，公正對待古老語言及其真正的模糊性呢？對吠陀詩人而言，河流與群山無疑與我們所見相同，但他們更突出地將其視為具有活動力的存在——因為在他們的語言中，凡以名稱涵括之物，唯有被理解為展現某種人類自身意識到的活動時，方能被掌握；若非被視為具有活動力，這些事物在他們心中便毫無趣味，也毫無存在可言。然而，從這種將自然某些部分視為具有活動力的觀念，到所謂的擬人化或神格化，仍有漫漫長路。即使詩人描述太陽立於戰車之上、身披金甲、伸展雙臂，這也不過是對自然中某種令他們聯想到自身行為之物的詩意感知。對我們而言是詩歌者，對他們卻是散文。對我們而言看似奇幻的意象，往往源於他們在掌握周遭世界時的無能為力，以及命名時的貧乏，而非出於震驚或取悅聽者的意圖。倘若我們能詢問婆私吒（Vasiṣṭha）或眾友（Viśvāmitra），或任何一位古老的雅利安詩人，問他們是否真的認為所見的金色圓球——太陽——是一個有手有腳、有心有肺的人，他們無疑會嘲笑我們，並告訴我們：儘管我們懂得他們的語言，卻不懂他們的思想。
像 *Savitṛ*（太陽）這樣的詞，最初的意思僅止於字面所表達的內容。它源自詞根 *su*（產生、賦予生命），因此當用來指稱太陽時，它只表達太陽在賦予生命與促進豐饒的行動中所被感知的那一部分，不多也不少。後來，*Savitṛ* 一方面成為某個神話人物的名稱，可以講述一些適用於賦予生機的太陽的故事；另一方面，*Savitṛ* 則退化為一個傳統而無意義的詞，僅用來指稱太陽。

我們在太陽的例子中所觀察到的過程，可以一再見諸大多數吠陀神祇。然而，並非所有神祇皆然。所謂的半神——河流、山巒、雲朵、海洋，以及其他如黎明、夜晚、風或暴風雨——從未升格至至高神祇的地位；但對於阿耆尼（Agni，火）、伐樓拿（Varuṇa，覆蓋之天）、因陀羅（Indra）、毗濕奴（Viṣṇu）、樓陀羅（Rudra）、蘇摩（Soma）、波闍尼耶（Parjanya）等神祇，則使用了種種稱號，並給予了完整的描述，這些描述在我們看來……

> 「對無限者的感知……是宗教的根源。」 在開始我們對宗教學的探究之前，有必要先釐清這門科學的範圍與方法。宗教學（the science of religion）並非神學，亦非護教學；它是一門純粹的歷史與比較科學，旨在研究宗教現象，如同語言科學（the science of language）研究語言現象一般。 正如語言科學揭示了語言的規律與發展，宗教學也試圖發現宗教的演進法則。我們必須區分兩種研究途徑：比較神學（comparative theology）與比較神話學（comparative mythology）。前者關注宗教的教義與倫理體系，後者則研究宗教的象徵與敘事形式。 值得注意的是，在吠陀（Veda）文獻中，我們發現了一種獨特的宗教形式，我稱之為單一神教（henotheism）或輪換主神教（kathenotheism）。這種信仰既非多神教（polytheism），亦非一神教（monotheism），而是輪流將每一位神祇視為至高無上的存在。例如，在梨俱吠陀（Rig-Veda）中，當讚頌阿耆尼（Agni）時，他便是唯一的神；當讚頌因陀羅（Indra）時，他亦成為唯一的神。這種「輪換主神教」的現象，正是宗教演化過程中的關鍵環節。 雅利安（Aryan）民族的宗教史尤其值得關注。從特尤斯（Dyaus）到提婆（Deva）的演變，顯示了語言如何影響宗教觀唸的形成。穆勒（Müller）曾指出，神話往往是「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）——當人們忘記了詞語的原始隱喻意義時，便產生了神話。例如，希臘人將「黎明」人格化為厄俄斯（Eos），便是這種語言疾病的典型例證。 閃語族（Semitic）與圖蘭語族（Turanian）的宗教傳統，則提供了另一種發展模式。透過比較這些不同語族的宗教文獻，我們得以追溯人類宗教意識的普遍規律。奧義書（Upanishad）中對「梵」（Brahman）的探討，與希臘哲學對「無限者」（the Infinite）的追尋，實有異曲同工之妙。 因此，宗教學的任務不僅是描述宗教現象，更要解釋其起源與發展。唯有透過嚴謹的比較方法，我們才能理解人類為何會產生宗教，以及宗教如何從最原始的形態演化至最高深的哲學體系。
**天空作為特尤斯（Dyaus），或稱「發光者」。**

弗里德里希‧馬克斯‧穆勒 讓我們審視另一位神祇的起源與歷史，這位神祇不僅是吠陀雅利安人最古老的神明之一，更是整個雅利安種族最古老的神明之一——我指的是吠陀中的特尤斯（Dyaus），希臘的宙斯（Ζεύς）。有些學者似乎仍對吠陀中存在這樣一位神祇存疑；誠然，在印度後期的文獻中，特尤斯作為神祇——甚至作為陽性名詞——已無跡可尋。在那裡，特尤斯已轉為陰性，僅意指「天空」。在我看來，吠陀研究者最驚人的發現之一，莫過於此：一位已知存在於希臘（Ζεὺς πατήρ）、義大利（Ju-piter）、《埃達》（Tyr）、日耳曼（Zio）的神祇，我們明知梵語中也應有其存在，卻始終未見蹤跡——如今竟在這些古老的吠陀頌詩中驟然顯現。在吠陀中，特尤斯不僅以陽性形式出現，更與「父親」（pitā）緊密相連，構成特尤斯‧皮塔（Dyaushpita）——此組合正與拉丁文的朱庇特（Jupiter）遙相呼應。這項特尤斯‧皮塔的發現，猶如最終透過強力望遠鏡，在我們先前經計算鎖定的天際方位，確切尋獲了那顆星辰。

然而，即使在吠陀中，特尤斯（Dyaus）也已是一顆逐漸黯淡的星辰。該詞的含義通常被解釋為「天空」，但其更精確的意義應是「明亮者」或「照耀者」，因為它源自詞根 *div* 或 *dyu*，意為「照耀、發光」；而這種照耀並照亮世界的活動，正是特尤斯之名所體現的內涵。這個「照耀者」究竟是誰，詞語本身並未明示。祂是一位阿修羅（asura），一位「活著的存在」——僅此而已。後來，特尤斯才成為神話故事的核心，而在日常語言中，它卻逐漸淪為一個傳統且無意義的「天空」詞彙，正如生命賦予者薩維特爾（Savitṛ）一般。
這個特尤斯（Dyaus），亦即天光或天穹的照耀者，無疑從最初便格外適合在諸提婆（deva）或光明存在者之間取得某種至高地位；我們知道，這種至高地位在希臘的宙斯（Zeus）與拉丁的朱比特（Jupiter）中如何徹底實現。在吠陀的「特尤斯」身上，我們同樣能觀察到這種傾向；然而，此處的傾向卻受到幾乎每位提婆（Deva）內在皆有的另一種傾向所制衡——即各自展現至高無上的特質。
特尤斯（Dyaus），即天空，經常與大地及火一同被祈求。例如（《梨俱吠陀》VI, 51, 5）：「特尤斯（天空），父親，與波哩提毗（Prithivī，大地），慈祥的母親，阿耆尼（Agni，火），兄弟，你們諸位善神（Vasus），你們這些光明者，請憐憫我們！」
特尤斯（Dyaus）顯然佔據首位，在這些古老祈禱文中通常也是如此。祂常被稱為父親。例如（I, 191, 6）：「特尤斯是父親，普利提維（Prithivi）大地是你們的母親；蘇摩（Soma）是你們的兄弟；阿底提（Aditi）是你們的姊妹。」又如（《梨俱吠陀》IV, i, 10）：「特尤斯，父親，創造者，特尤斯‧庇塔（Dyaush pitā，即希臘文Ζεὺς πατὴρ γενετήρ）。」
然而，特尤斯（Dyaus，天空）更常與大地（Prithivī）一同被祈求；這兩個詞結合起來，在吠陀中形成一種雙重神祇，稱為 Dyāvāpṛthivī，即天與地。
如今，在《吠陀》中有許多段落將天與地視為至高神祇來祈求。因此，諸神被說成是祂們的兒子¹，尤其是《吠陀》中最受歡迎的兩位神祇——因陀羅²與阿耆尼³——被提及為祂們的後裔。正是這對父母創造了世界⁴，保護著它⁵，並以其力量支撐一切存在之物⁶。
然而，在天地被賦予一切可想像的詞語來表達其不朽、全能與永恆之後，我們突然聽聞諸神中有一位巧匠創造了天地，無論其名為特尤斯與普里提維（Dyavdprithivi）或科達西（Kodasi）。在某些地方，因陀羅（Indra）被說成是產生並支撐天地者，而同一因陀羅在其他地方又被描繪為特尤斯之子，或天地之子。
事實上，我們在此首次見到某種¹梨俱吠陀（Rig-Veda, I, 159, i, devaputre）²同書（Ibid., IV, 17）的記載。
3 同上，X, 2, 7，yam tvā dyāvāpṛthivī yam tvā apaḥ, tvaṣṭā yam tvā sugraṇī mā gagana.
4 同上，I, 159, 2，suretasa pitara bhuma kakraiuh。
同上，I, 1 60, 2，pita mata ka bhuvanani rakshataA。
